`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"To demonstrate that we understand how to test in production, it\u2019s a good idea to mention at least one of these methods. Let\u2019s briefly review shadow deployment and A/B testing."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.h5,{id:"shadow-deployment",children:"Shadow deployment"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In this method, we deploy the new model in parallel with the existing model. Each incoming request is routed to both models, but only the existing model's prediction is served to the user."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"By shadow deploying the model, we minimize the risk of unreliable predictions until the newly developed model has been thoroughly tested. However, this is a costly approach that doubles the number of predictions."}),`

`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"With this method, we deploy the new model in parallel with the existing model. A portion of the traffic is routed to the newly developed model, while the remaining requests are routed to the existing model."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In order to execute A/B testing correctly, there are two important factors to consider. First, the traffic routed to each model has to be random. Second, A/B tests should be run on a sufficient number of data points in order for the results to be legitimate."}),`

`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"To serve requests in production, we need a prediction pipeline. An important design decision to make is the choice between online prediction and batch prediction."}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(t.p,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(t.strong,{children:"Batch prediction."})," With batch prediction, the model makes predictions periodically. `,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"There are various reasons why an ML system may fail after it is deployed to production. One of the most common reasons is data distribution shift."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Data distribution shift refers to the scenario in which the data a model encounters in production differs from that it encountered during training. `,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In many ML interviews, you won\u2019t be asked infrastructure-related questions. However, some ML roles, such as DevOps and MLOps may require infrastructure knowledge. So, it\u2019s important to be clear about the interviewer\u2019s expectations for this topic."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Infrastructure is a very broad subject and cannot be summarized in just a few lines. If you\u2019re interested in learning more about ML infrastructure, refer to [44][45][46]."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.h3,{id:"summary",children:"Summary"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"In this chapter, we proposed a framework for an ML system design interview. While many topics discussed in this chapter are task-specific, some are generic and applicable to a wide range of tasks. Throughout this book, we only focus on unique talking points specific to the problem at hand, in order to avoid repetition. For example, topics related to deployment, monitoring, and infrastructure are often similar, regardless of the task. Therefore, we do not repeat generic topics in later chapters, but you are usually expected to talk about them during an interview."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Finally, no engineer can be an expert in every aspect of the ML lifecycle. Some engineers specialize in deployment and production, while others specialize in model development. Some companies may not care about infrastructure, while others may focus heavily on monitoring and infrastructure. Data science roles generally require more data engineering, while applied ML roles focus more on model development and productionization. Depending on the role and the interviewer's preference, some steps may be discussed in more detail, while others may be discussed briefly or even skipped. In general, a candidate should seek to drive the conversation, while being ready to go with the interviewer\u2019s flow, if they raise a question."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(t.p,{children:"Now you understand these fundamentals, we\u2019re ready to tackle some of the most common ML system design interview questions."}),`
